patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Open with: “I’ve had this itchy rash and low fever since I got back from my trip.” Volunteer early: itchy red rash started on arms and spread to chest/legs, low-grade on-and-off fever, red/irritated eyes, fatigue and body aches. Mention you had mosquito bites on the trip. If asked, add: headache behind eyes, joint pain (wrists/ankles), muscle soreness, mild nausea/low appetite, swollen neck glands, and that symptoms began ~5 days after returning. Withhold unless directly asked: trying to get pregnant/no reliable birth control, unprotected sex during/after trip, multiple daytime mosquito bites with little repellent, brief tingling in hands that resolved. Be resistant/embarrassed if asked about sexual history, pregnancy, or urinary symptoms; only admit unprotected sex with a new partner, worry you might be pregnant (no test yet), and mild burning with urination if the clinician is specific and nonjudgmental. Express misconceptions: worry it’s measles, allergy from new detergent, or dengue; wonder if ibuprofen worsened the rash. If met with empathy, soften, share more, and ask about costs/reporting and missing unpaid work.

medications: Prenatal vitamin (with folic acid 0.8 mg) 1 tablet daily
Sertraline 50 mg orally daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3 g/day)
confounders: Recent travel to mosquito-endemic region with multiple arboviruses circulating
Prior dengue exposure (IgG positive) increasing risk of serologic cross-reactivity with Zika tests
Early pregnancy symptoms (fatigue, mild nausea) overlapping with viral prodrome
Use of acetaminophen before presentation masking peak fever

correct_diagnosis.primary: Zika virus infectious disease
acceptable_alternatives: Arboviral illness consistent with Zika (pending confirmatory testing)
required_evidence: Compatible acute illness (mild fever, maculopapular rash, arthralgia, nonpurulent conjunctivitis, headache, myalgia) with recent travel to or residence in an area with Zika transmission or sexual exposure to a potentially infected partner
Exclusion/consideration of key differentials with overlapping presentations (especially dengue and chikungunya)
No signs of severe disease requiring admission (e.g., hypotension/shock, severe bleeding, severe abdominal pain, altered mental status)
Laboratory pattern compatible with viral illness (often normal or mild leukopenia and/or thrombocytopenia; mild transaminase elevation possible)
Appropriate confirmatory testing planned or performed per timing (NAAT/RT-PCR early; IgM later with confirmatory PRNT where indicated)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal or mildly low (mild leukopenia possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal or mildly low; marked thrombocytopenia would raise concern for dengue
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - Leukocyte esterase: Negative (pyuria would suggest alternate diagnosis such as UTI)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever or pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care: oral hydration, rest
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin until dengue is reasonably excluded due to bleeding risk
Mosquito bite prevention during the first week of illness to reduce onward transmission (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets, stay in screened/air-conditioned areas)

Sexual transmission prevention counseling: use condoms/abstain; if male with possible Zika exposure, use condoms/abstain for 3 months; if female, for 2 months (from symptom onset or last possible exposure)
Pregnancy considerations: assess pregnancy status and desire for pregnancy; if pregnant, urgent referral/coordination with obstetrics for Zika testing and fetal surveillance per guidelines
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours if symptoms worsen or new red flags occur; otherwise follow up in 3-7 days for symptom check and to review/arrange Zika/dengue/chikungunya testing results and pregnancy-related planning as applicable

must_include: Zika is usually a self-limited viral illness; symptoms often last several days to 1 week
Diagnosis is based on symptoms plus exposure risk; confirmatory testing depends on timing since symptom onset
Important overlap with dengue/chikungunya and why this affects medication choices (bleeding risk with dengue)
Supportive care only (no routine antiviral therapy)
Use acetaminophen for fever/pain; avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded
Hydration and rest; when to seek urgent care (severe bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fainting, confusion, shortness of breath, signs of dehydration)
Transmission routes: mosquito-borne and sexual transmission; low risk from casual contact
Pregnancy risks: potential fetal harm (e.g., microcephaly and other congenital anomalies) and need for pregnancy testing/OB follow-up if pregnant or trying to conceive
Expected course and prognosis; most recover fully
Mosquito avoidance measures to prevent spreading virus to others
Sexual precautions and recommended duration of condom use/abstinence after exposure/symptoms
Avoid donating blood/semen for the recommended period after possible Zika exposure

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 4-year-old boy. You seem anxious and speak vaguely; a parent answers most questions but you can add short phrases. Start by saying: “My hands feel stiff and sore and I keep dropping things.” If asked about other problems, add: shoulders/hips feel tight, you tire easily, you’ve always been short and seem more hunched.

Only if specifically asked, mention: lots of cavities/dental problems, blurry light-sensitive vision slowly worsening, short of breath on stairs with occasional racing heart, loud snoring with gasping, hearing trouble (worse in noise), frequent ear/sinus infections.

Do not volunteer hidden issues unless directly prompted: can’t fully straighten fingers/elbows; joints feel “stuck”; face looks puffier/coarser in photos; belly looks bigger/bloated; nighttime tingling/numb thumb/first two fingers relieved by shaking; told you have a heart murmur.

Resist sharing bedwetting/leaks and shame about teeth unless gently normalized and asked privately.

Express misconceptions: you/parent suspect rheumatoid arthritis; breathing is asthma/out of shape; vision just needs stronger glasses.

If clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly and share one extra detail. Parent worries insurance won’t cover genetic tests/specialists/DME and fears disability support affects jobs/insurance.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis (lysosomal storage disorder; most consistent with mucolipidosis type II/III spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: Lysosomal storage disorder (unspecified) pending confirmatory testing
required_evidence: Progressive multisystem features typical of lysosomal storage disease (e.g., coarse facial features, skeletal dysplasia/joint stiffness, developmental delay, organomegaly, recurrent respiratory/ear infections)
Radiographic evidence of dysostosis multiplex (if available) or clinical skeletal abnormalities (short stature, thoracic deformity, contractures)
Enzyme testing pattern suggestive of mucolipidosis (multiple lysosomal enzymes elevated in plasma with relative deficiency in fibroblasts/leukocytes) or confirmed GNPTAB/GNPTG pathogenic variants
Exclusion of common mimics (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses) via appropriate screening (urine GAGs) if performed

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal or mild anemia; no disease-specific pattern
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be elevated if concurrent infection/inflammation
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Normal to mildly elevated (can be mildly elevated with hepatic involvement)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Alkaline: May be elevated (bone turnover/skeletal disease can raise ALP)

medications: Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO every 4-6 hours as needed for pain or fever (max 75 mg/kg/day; do not exceed 4 g/day)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain or inflammation (avoid if dehydration, renal disease, or GI bleeding risk)
non_pharmacological: Genetics/metabolic referral for confirmatory testing (GNPTAB/GNPTG sequencing; enzyme studies as available) and family counseling
Supportive multidisciplinary care: physical therapy/occupational therapy for contractures and mobility; speech/swallow evaluation if feeding issues; nutrition support
Orthopedics evaluation for dysostosis multiplex/hip dysplasia/scoliosis; consider assistive devices
Cardiology assessment (echocardiogram) for valvular disease/cardiomyopathy surveillance
Pulmonology/ENT evaluation for recurrent infections, airway issues, hearing loss; immunizations up to date; consider sleep study if symptoms of OSA
Avoid unnecessary hepatotoxic exposures; monitor growth, development, and functional status over time
Discuss that there is no established curative enzyme replacement therapy for mucolipidosis II/III; management is symptom-directed; consider clinical trials when available
Advance care planning appropriate to severity and family goals (especially in severe early-onset disease)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after specialty referrals are initiated, then every 3-6 months (or sooner for acute respiratory/ENT issues or functional decline).

must_include: Mucolipidosis is a genetic lysosomal storage disorder causing buildup of substances in many organs
Symptoms are multisystem and progressive; severity varies (II more severe/earlier, III milder/later)
Diagnosis requires specialized genetic/enzyme testing; routine labs may be nonspecific
No definitive curative therapy is standard; treatment focuses on supportive, multidisciplinary symptom management
Plan for referrals (genetics/metabolic, orthopedics, cardiology, ENT/pulmonology, PT/OT, speech/nutrition)
Pain/fever options and safe dosing; when to avoid NSAIDs (dehydration/renal risk)
Discuss prognosis uncertainty until subtype/severity clarified; outline expected monitoring
Offer genetic counseling for recurrence risk and family planning options
Provide guidance on red flags requiring urgent care (breathing difficulty, recurrent severe infections, feeding/aspiration signs, worsening mobility, new cardiac symptoms)
Importance of routine vaccinations and prompt treatment of respiratory/ear infections
Home strategies to support mobility and function (therapy exercises, safe activity, assistive devices as needed)
Medication safety: avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to prior maculopapular rash; inform all clinicians of this allergy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: presenting symptoms, onset/progression, developmental history, recurrent infections, feeding/aspiration, sleep/breathing, pain, family history/consanguinity, prior evaluations, current meds, and prior rash to amoxicillin-clavulanate.

Order initial evaluation and targeted tests to support suspicion and assess organ involvement (baseline CBC/CMP, urinalysis, imaging as indicated, echocardiogram, pulmonary/ENT evaluation) and initiate referral-based confirmatory testing (genetics/lysosomal enzyme panels appropriate for mucolipidosis subtype workup).
Review results to identify multisystem involvement, exclude common alternative diagnoses, and justify referral/confirmatory testing; recognize routine labs may be nonspecific.
Communicate suspected/working diagnosis of mucolipidosis and the need for specialized confirmatory genetic/enzyme testing; avoid over-certainty if results are not confirmatory.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (supportive multidisciplinary care), address concerns (prognosis uncertainty, genetic counseling, red flags), and educate lifestyle (vaccines, infection management, mobility supports, medication allergy safety).
Before any prescribing, confirm/document allergy history including prior maculopapular rash to amoxicillin-clavulanate and any severe reactions.
Before prescribing, screen for interactions with current medications/supplements (noncritical but expected when prescribing).
Provide safe symptomatic treatment (e.g., acetaminophen; NSAIDs only if appropriate) and avoid contraindicated agents; ensure dosing guidance and monitoring instructions.
Arrange timely follow-up and referrals (genetics/metabolic, cardiology, orthopedics, ENT/pulmonology, PT/OT, speech/nutrition) and set monitoring plan.
Close after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

Start by saying: “I’ve been having really bad pain in my chest and back, and I can’t catch my breath.” Volunteer: severe aching chest/upper back pain worsening over 1 day, short of breath with minimal walking, feels like a pain crisis but worse, very low energy. Start with: “I’ve had an itchy red rash and achy joints since I got back from my trip.” Volunteer: rash began on arms and spread to chest/legs; wrists/ankles sore and stiff; low-grade fever with chills; eyes red/irritated. correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
required_evidence: Chronic fluctuating fatigable weakness beginning in childhood or adolescence (often limb-girdle and/or ocular/bulbar involvement) without sensory loss
Clinical fatigability (worsens with exertion, improves with rest) and/or objective decrement on repetitive nerve stimulation or abnormal single-fiber EMG
Absence of typical autoimmune myasthenia features (e.g., no thymoma history; AChR/MuSK/LRP4 antibodies negative if tested)
History of worsening weakness/respiratory symptoms with macrolides (supports neuromuscular transmission vulnerability)

Genetic confirmation of glycosylation-pathway CMS (e.g., biallelic pathogenic variants in GMPPB/GFPT1/DPM genes) OR characteristic neuromuscular transmission study pattern consistent with CMS when genetics pending

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected from CMS itself)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Bicarbon: Typically normal; may be low only if significant hyperventilation/respiratory alkalosis during distress (not expected at baseline)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake (titrate by response and tolerability; typical range 30–60 mg per dose)
3,4-Diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily (titrate cautiously; do not exceed 80 mg/day; specialist-guided)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or equivalent long-acting beta-agonist regimen) as adjunct in selected CMS subtypes; specialist-guided
non_pharmacological: Neuromuscular specialist referral for subtype-directed therapy and genetic counseling

Avoid medications that can worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium-containing products, certain beta-blockers) unless benefits outweigh risks and with close monitoring
Respiratory assessment if bulbar/respiratory symptoms: bedside spirometry (FVC/NIF) and clear escalation plan for worsening dyspnea
Physical therapy/energy conservation strategies; fall-risk assessment if proximal weakness
Discuss vaccination and infection prevention; early treatment of respiratory infections with non-macrolide, non-penicillin alternatives when needed (based on infection site and local guidelines)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1–2 weeks for symptom response and adverse effects after starting/titrating therapy; sooner (24–72 hours) if bulbar or respiratory symptoms worsen; ongoing neuromuscular follow-up every 3–6 months

must_include: Condition is genetic (congenital) and affects neuromuscular junction signaling rather than autoimmune attack
Symptoms can fluctuate and worsen with exertion, illness, or certain medications
Subtype (glycosylation defect) matters because response to medications differs; genetics and specialized testing guide therapy
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness; may require trial-and-titration of agents
How to take pyridostigmine (timing with activities, expected onset, common side effects like abdominal cramps/diarrhea/salivation)
Amifampridine risks and precautions (dose titration, seizure risk especially with predisposition or interacting drugs; do not exceed max daily dose)
When to seek urgent care: new/worsening shortness of breath, trouble swallowing, inability to clear secretions, or rapidly progressive weakness
Medication allergies noted: azithromycin caused worsening weakness/shortness of breath; penicillin caused urticarial rash—avoid and document clearly
Provide a list of drug classes to avoid or use cautiously (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, certain beta-blockers) and advise checking before starting new meds
Discuss realistic expectations: improvement is common but not always complete; long-term management and monitoring are needed
Energy conservation, pacing, and planned rest to reduce fatigability
Illness plan: infections can exacerbate weakness—seek early evaluation and avoid contraindicated antibiotics
Safety planning for swallowing/aspiration risk if bulbar symptoms (eat slowly, upright posture, consider swallow evaluation if choking/coughing with meals)

Review results to support diagnosis and ensure no alternative explanation or contraindicating abnormalities (e.g., electrolyte derangements that could raise seizure risk).

Initiate/adjust symptomatic therapy appropriate for CMS with careful titration: pyridostigmine trial if appropriate and/or amifampridine with strict max daily dose guidance; include dosing schedule, titration plan, and adverse effect monitoring.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (genetic, NMJ, fluctuation, subtype relevance), explain treatment (goals, pyridostigmine timing/side effects, amifampridine precautions/seizure risk/max dose, urgent symptoms), address concerns (document allergies, avoid/caution drug list, expectations), and lifestyle/illness/swallow safety planning.
Arrange follow-up to assess response and side effects, adjust dosing, review genetic results, and reinforce avoidance list; provide earlier review if bulbar/respiratory symptoms.
Close encounter after safety-netting and follow-up plan are established.

HIV confirmatory differentiation assay (or equivalent confirmatory test per local algorithm)
CD4 count
HIV genotype resistance testing (RT/PR; consider integrase if available/indicated)
CBC, CMP (including creatinine/eGFR, AST/ALT, bilirubin)

Hepatitis C antibody (with reflex RNA if positive)
STI screening (syphilis, gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT as indicated)
TB screening (IGRA or TST) as indicated
Pregnancy test if applicable
Fasting lipids and A1c (baseline metabolic risk)

tool_call_sequence.description: Orders confirmatory/staging tests and baseline labs required to start ART safely and to screen common coinfections.
Retrieves key results needed to communicate diagnosis and avoid contraindicated ART choices (e.g., renal impairment, pregnancy considerations, HBV coinfection).

required_args.diagnosis: HIV infection (immunodeficiency) pending/with confirmatory testing; assess stage by CD4 and viral load

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the working diagnosis and frames staging/assessment.

tool_call_sequence.description: Critical safety check before prescribing ART to prevent allergic reactions (e.g., prior hypersensitivity to components).

medications_considered: integrase inhibitor-based ART (bictegravir or dolutegravir regimens)

tool_call_sequence.description: Critical safety check to identify significant drug-drug interactions (e.g., polyvalent cations with INSTIs, rifampin).

considerations: HBV coinfection (avoid stopping HBV-active agents; avoid 2-drug DTG/3TC if HBV present)
need for opportunistic infection prophylaxis based on CD4

tool_call_sequence.description: Critical safety check before prescribing to ensure chosen regimen is appropriate given comorbidities and coinfections.

required_args.medication: First-line INSTI-based ART regimen appropriate to labs/contraindications
required_args.sig: Once daily as indicated by selected regimen
notes: Start promptly while awaiting genotype if clinically appropriate per guidelines
Provide adherence counseling and side effect guidance
If HBV positive, ensure regimen includes two HBV-active NRTIs (TAF/TDF + FTC/3TC)

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates effective, guideline-concordant ART with correct regimen constraints and dosing.
Explains lifelong ART, adherence, side effects/urgent symptoms, harm reduction, safer sex, and follow-up/lab monitoring.

followup_items: review viral load/CD4 and baseline labs
review genotype results and adjust ART if needed
vaccination review (HAV, HBV, HPV, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19, etc.)

tool_call_sequence.description: Ensures timely monitoring for treatment response, safety labs, resistance results, and preventive care.
Closes the encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent clearly states that the HIV test is positive and indicates HIV infection, while acknowledging confirmatory testing per algorithm is needed.
Agent explains CD4 count as a measure of immune strength and that lower CD4 increases risk for opportunistic infections; also explains viral load as amount of virus and treatment target.
Agent recommends starting ART promptly and frames ART as lifelong, highly effective therapy with goals of undetectable viral load and immune recovery.
Agent provides a plain-language once-daily medication-taking plan and emphasizes adherence, what to do if a dose is missed, and avoiding stopping ART without medical advice.

Agent discusses common side effects (e.g., GI upset, headache, insomnia) and gives clear urgent return precautions (e.g., severe rash, jaundice, shortness of breath, severe abdominal pain, signs of allergic reaction).
Agent addresses confidentiality, offers support resources (case management, counseling, community organizations), and uses non-stigmatizing language.
Agent discusses partner testing and notification options (patient referral, provider-assisted/anonymous notification where available) and offers STI screening.

Agent explains U=U accurately: with sustained undetectable viral load, risk of sexual transmission is effectively zero, while noting this does not prevent other STIs and requires ongoing adherence and monitoring.
Agent provides safer sex and harm-reduction counseling (condoms, PrEP for partners as applicable, avoid sharing needles, substance-use support if relevant).
Agent orders or confirms appropriate baseline labs (viral load, CD4, genotype, CBC/CMP, HBV/HCV, STI/TB screening, pregnancy test if applicable) and uses results to guide regimen selection.
Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents all three critical safety checks: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error.

Agent avoids contraindicated regimens (e.g., does not use dolutegravir/lamivudine if HBV coinfection is present/unknown, or if high viral load/low CD4 where not appropriate per local guideline) and selects renal-appropriate tenofovir formulation.
Agent arranges follow-up within 2–4 weeks (sooner if advanced disease) for lab review and ART optimization, and provides a monitoring plan.

minimal_paths.description: Rapid-start ART after confirming positive test context, ordering baseline labs, and completing safety checks; provide required counseling and follow-up.
must_collect: HBV history or risk (or plan to test)
steps: ASK (focused history including meds, allergies, comorbidities, pregnancy potential, exposures)
ORDER_LAB (viral load, CD4, genotype, CBC/CMP, HBV/HCV, STI ± TB, pregnancy if applicable)
EDUCATE (explain_diagnosis + address_concerns including U=U and confidentiality)
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION (renal function, HBV, pregnancy, other key contraindications)
PRESCRIBE (guideline first-line INSTI-based regimen; HBV-active if needed)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (2–4 weeks; review labs/genotype; monitoring plan)

minimal_paths.description: Obtain/verify confirmatory testing and key baseline results before prescribing, then start ART with safety checks and counseling.
steps: ORDER_LAB (include confirmatory test if not already done, viral load, CD4, CMP/renal, HBV)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent retrieves results earlier to tailor counseling (e.g., discusses specific CD4 value and whether OI prophylaxis may be needed) before prescribing.

Agent includes additional prevention actions (vaccination counseling, PrEP discussion for HIV-negative partners) within EDUCATE without changing required safety checks or core sequence.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly, but anxious because the episodes feel like a stroke. Start with: “I keep getting these migraines where one side of my body goes weak and numb, and it really scares me.” Early in the visit, volunteer: one-sided headache building over hours; one-sided arm/face weakness; tingling/numbness spreading from hand up arm; light/sound sensitivity; nausea/vomiting. If asked about warning signs/neurologic symptoms, share: flashing zig-zag lights or blind spot before headache, brief slurred speech/word-finding trouble, weakness lasting hours (once into next day), triggers (poor sleep, stress, skipped meals), history since late teens with increased frequency. Do not mention unless specifically asked: irritability/fatigue before attacks, neck stiffness/soreness, “hungover” day after, dizziness/off-balance, blurry vision in one eye. Be hesitant to admit you avoid the ER due to fear of being judged/drug-seeking; reveal only if asked about care-seeking. Express misconceptions: worry it’s a stroke, pinched nerve, or low blood sugar. If clinician is empathic, you open up and ask about costs/insurance and missing work.

medications: Verapamil ER 120 mg PO nightly
Acetazolamide 250 mg PO twice daily
Naproxen 500 mg PO as needed at headache onset (max 1000 mg/day)
Metoclopramide 10 mg PO/IV as needed for nausea
Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO daily
Levonorgestrel/ethinyl estradiol 0.1 mg/20 mcg PO daily (combined oral contraceptive)
confounders: Recurrent unilateral weakness and aphasia during attacks mimicking transient ischemic attack/ischemic stroke
Use of estrogen-containing combined oral contraceptive increases concern for thrombotic events when presenting with focal neurologic deficits
Family history of early-onset stroke in a first-degree relative (reported) complicates triage and risk stratification
Mild hypomagnesemia may exacerbate migraine frequency/severity and can be mistaken as a primary cause of neurologic symptoms
Anxiety/panic symptoms during aura (hyperventilation, paresthesias) can mimic or amplify neurologic complaints
Acetazolamide can cause paresthesias and fatigue, potentially confounding sensory symptoms during aura

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM)
acceptable_alternatives: Hemiplegic migraine (sporadic hemiplegic migraine if no family history)
Migraine with aura (if no motor weakness is ultimately confirmed)
required_evidence: Recurrent migraine attacks with aura that includes fully reversible motor weakness (hemiparesis) plus at least one other reversible aura symptom (visual/sensory/speech-language)
Headache temporally associated with aura (during aura or within 60 minutes)
No better explanation such as acute ischemic stroke/TIA, seizure with postictal paralysis, intracranial hemorrhage, or CNS infection
Family history of similar hemiplegic migraine in a first- or second-degree relative (supports familial subtype)

medications: Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO once, then 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed (max 3000 mg/day if frequent use or liver risk)
Naproxen 500 mg PO once, then 250-500 mg PO every 12 hours as needed with food (avoid if NSAID contraindications)
Metoclopramide 10 mg PO/IV every 8 hours as needed for nausea
Magnesium sulfate 1-2 g IV once (consider for acute migraine with aura; monitor for hypotension/flushing)
non_pharmacological: Treat as possible stroke/TIA until excluded: urgent neurologic assessment and appropriate emergent workup per setting
Rest in dark/quiet room; hydration; avoid driving/operating machinery during neurologic deficits
Trigger management: regular sleep, meals, hydration; limit alcohol; manage stress; avoid known triggers
Headache diary and aura symptom tracking; counsel on medication-overuse headache avoidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (sooner/ED evaluation immediately if first episode, atypical features, prolonged deficits, or new/worsening neurologic symptoms)

must_include: Hemiplegic migraine can cause temporary one-sided weakness and other aura symptoms that are reversible
Because symptoms can mimic stroke, first-time or atypical episodes require urgent evaluation to rule out dangerous causes
Familial form is supported by similar episodes in close relatives and may have genetic contribution
Use non-opioid acute treatments (acetaminophen/NSAID) and antiemetic as needed; early treatment is more effective
Avoid vasoconstrictive migraine abortives (triptans/ergots) in hemiplegic migraine due to safety concerns
Review when to seek emergency care: new or persistent weakness, speech difficulty, severe sudden headache, confusion, seizure, or symptoms lasting longer than usual
Confirm allergy history: sulfonamide antibiotics caused urticarial rash; prochlorperazine caused dystonic reaction
Discuss side effects and precautions for chosen medications (NSAID GI/renal risk; metoclopramide risk of akathisia/dystonia and to stop/seek care if abnormal movements occur)
Shared plan for what to do if symptoms worsen or do not resolve as expected
Identify and avoid triggers; maintain regular sleep and meals; adequate hydration
Limit acute medication use to avoid medication-overuse headache
Keep a headache diary and bring it to neurology follow-up; discuss preventive therapy options with neurology if attacks are frequent/severe

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 27, a warehouse picker on shifts. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about cost and missing work. Start by saying: tiny cuts bleed a lot and take forever to stop; a shaving nick oozes for hours; you bruise easily; gums bleed when brushing.

Only give more details if directly asked: nosebleeds lasting 20–30 minutes, heavy periods >7 days with clots and frequent pad changes, bleeding that restarts hours to days later (e.g., after a tooth pull), deep painful swellings after bumps, and headaches after minor hits that worry you.

Do not mention unless specifically probed about childhood/newborn history, miscarriages, joint pressure after twists, or repeat urgent care visits for re-bleeding.

Resist bringing up post-sex spotting, avoiding dental work, and hiding period bleeding; if asked gently, admit embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: assume it’s low iron; mention family “hemophilia,” unsure type; wonder if platelets are “weak” or it’s a clotting-factor/APTT issue.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften, share a bit more, but keep returning to fears about bills, insurance denial, and losing your job.

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Tranexamic acid 1 g PO three times daily as needed during heavy menses (up to 5 days)
Factor XIII concentrate 40 IU/kg IV every 4 weeks (prophylaxis)
confounders: Normal PT/INR and aPTT despite significant bleeding tendency
Heavy menstrual bleeding causing iron deficiency anemia, which may be misattributed to gynecologic causes alone
Intermittent NSAID use for dysmenorrhea can worsen mucosal bleeding/bruising
Prior use of combined oral contraceptive in the past (can mask severity of menstrual bleeding)

differentials: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital factor XIII deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII
required_evidence: History of bleeding with minor or no trauma, including delayed/recurrent bleeding after injury or procedures
Normal PT/INR despite clinically significant bleeding (supports FXIII deficiency over vitamin K–dependent factor deficiencies and common-pathway factor deficiencies)
Normal platelet count (argues against thrombocytopenia as cause of bleeding)
Anemia may be present if bleeding is chronic/recurrent (low hemoglobin/hematocrit possible)
Need for confirmatory testing not provided in available labs: factor XIII activity assay (low) and/or clot solubility test (positive)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be decreased if ongoing/recurrent bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be decreased if ongoing/recurrent bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.RBC count: May be decreased if significant blood loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.MCV: Often normal; may be low if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding
expected_lab_results.RDW: May be increased if iron deficiency develops; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.PT/INR: Normal (key expected finding in factor XIII deficiency)

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (plasma-derived or recombinant) 35 IU/kg IV once for acute bleeding; repeat dosing guided by clinical response and factor XIII activity if available
If factor XIII concentrate unavailable: Cryoprecipitate 1 unit/10 kg IV once (or 10 units adult dose) for acute bleeding
If cryoprecipitate unavailable: Fresh frozen plasma 15-20 mL/kg IV once for acute bleeding
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology consultation for definitive diagnosis confirmation and long-term prophylaxis planning
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and other platelet-inhibiting agents; use acetaminophen for pain/fever
Bleeding precautions and prompt evaluation for head injury or neurologic symptoms due to risk of intracranial hemorrhage
Plan for peri-procedural prophylaxis (FXIII replacement) before surgeries/dental work
Assess for iron deficiency and treat if present (based on CBC indices and clinical context)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1 week; sooner (same day/24 hours) if active bleeding, anemia, or any concern for intracranial hemorrhage

must_include: Bleeding is likely due to a clot-stabilizing factor problem (factor XIII), which can occur even when PT/INR is normal

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 38-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you keep getting sudden “exploding” headaches that peak within seconds (thunderclap), usually starting in the back of your head and spreading. Volunteer that bright light and noise worsen it, it comes in waves with several attacks this past week, and you feel nauseated during the worst pain.

If asked, add that attacks often start with exertion (straining in the bathroom, sex, heavy lifting), your neck feels stiff/sore, you sometimes see shimmering spots or brief blurry vision, and you’ve had brief episodes of arm tingling/weakness and short confusion/word-finding trouble that resolved. Mention you checked your BP once and it was higher during an attack.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: recent postpartum/pregnancy loss, triptan use, decongestant use, heavy energy drinks/caffeine, missed pituitary hormone doses. Only admit cannabis use and trying a friend’s stimulant pill if specifically asked about substances.

Express fear it’s “the worst headache of my life” (aneurysm/bleed), but also wonder if it’s migraines or sinus infection. Share cost/work worries and preference to avoid scans. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily
Desmopressin 0.1 mg PO at bedtime
Estradiol/norethindrone 1 mg/0.5 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Hypopituitarism due to Rathke's cleft (pouch) cyst
confounders: Migraine history with sensory hypersensitivity (phonophobia) mimicking thunderclap headache syndromes
Recent postpartum state within the last 6 weeks (risk factor for RCVS and may mimic preeclampsia-related headache)
Use of estrogen-containing hormone therapy (potential vasoactive trigger)
Hyponatremia from desmopressin use can contribute to headache and nausea, clouding neurologic assessment
Intermittent severe hypertension during pain episodes may suggest hypertensive emergency or pheochromocytoma

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
acceptable_alternatives: Thunderclap headache syndrome (pending vascular imaging confirmation)
Migraine with phonophobia (if no thunderclap headache and normal vascular imaging)
required_evidence: Acute severe headache pattern (often thunderclap), with/without focal neurologic deficits or seizures
Neurovascular imaging showing multifocal segmental cerebral arterial vasoconstriction (CTA/MRA/DSA) OR high clinical suspicion with plan for repeat imaging in 1–2 weeks if initial imaging is normal
Exclusion of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage and other secondary causes (e.g., cervical artery dissection, CNS infection)
Clinical course compatible with reversibility (improvement and/or resolution of vasoconstriction on follow-up imaging within ~12 weeks)
Identification of potential triggers (postpartum state; exposure to vasoactive substances such as triptans, SSRIs/SNRIs, decongestants, cannabis, cocaine/amphetamines) when present

expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal (no thrombocytopenia suggesting other systemic process)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Sodium: Usually normal (monitor for hyponatremia if severe headache, vomiting, or SIADH suspected)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - BUN: Normal (assess baseline renal function surrogate prior to contrast imaging/IV meds)

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day if no liver disease)
non_pharmacological: Urgent neuroimaging and evaluation: non-contrast head CT (if acute severe headache) and CTA/MRA head/neck; consider lumbar puncture if SAH not excluded by imaging/timing
Avoid vasoactive/trigger substances (triptans, ergots, decongestants, stimulants, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis; review serotonergic agents and other sympathomimetics)
Blood pressure management: avoid severe hypertension; avoid hypotension that could worsen cerebral perfusion (individualized targets per neurology)
Neurology consultation and consider inpatient monitoring if thunderclap headache, neurologic deficits, seizure, or imaging abnormalities (ischemia/hemorrhage)
If initial vascular imaging is normal but suspicion remains, arrange repeat CTA/MRA in 1–2 weeks to detect evolving vasoconstriction
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1 week; repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks if needed and again by 12 weeks to document reversibility

must_include: RCVS involves transient narrowing/spasm of brain arteries that can cause sudden severe headaches and sometimes stroke/bleeding
Diagnosis is supported by vascular imaging and confirmed by improvement/resolution over weeks
Need to rule out dangerous mimics (especially aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage)
Calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is used to reduce vasoconstriction and headache burden; it is not an antibiotic
Pain control plan and avoidance of medications that can worsen vasoconstriction (triptans/ergots, decongestants, stimulants)
Potential side effects to watch for (dizziness, low blood pressure) and when to seek urgent care
Return precautions: new/worsening thunderclap headache, weakness/numbness, trouble speaking, vision changes, seizure, fainting, persistent vomiting
Discuss prognosis: often improves over weeks, but complications (ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage) can occur and require prompt evaluation
Avoid vasoactive substances and recreational drugs; review OTC cold medications for decongestants
Hydration, sleep, and stress reduction; avoid extreme exertion/sexual activity if it reliably triggers thunderclap headaches until cleared by neurology
Adherence to follow-up imaging/appointments to document reversibility

State diagnosis as Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to point mutation and document key differentials considered and why genetic/phenotypic data support current diagnosis.

Re-confirm allergy status immediately before prescribing if any new information emerged; ensures safety check is temporally linked to prescribing.
Optional repeat interaction check immediately before prescribing if medication list changed during the visit.

Initiate evidence-based pharmacotherapy aligned with risks and labs (e.g., GLP-1 RA for weight/appetite and glycemic risk; metformin for insulin resistance; statin for dyslipidemia), including titration and side-effect plan.
Arrange monitoring plan and follow-up timeline; include weight/appetite tracking and repeat A1c/glucose, lipids, ALT; reinforce shared decision-making and long-term structure.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly explains that the condition is genetic and causes a Prader-Willi-like pattern of hyperphagia, weight gain, and behavioral/endocrine issues, and that it is not due to willpower or parenting.

The agent discusses key differentials (e.g., classic Prader-Willi syndrome due to imprinting/deletion/UPD, hypothalamic obesity, endocrine disorders such as hypothyroidism/Cushing syndrome, medication-induced weight gain) and states why the point mutation/genetic testing plus phenotype supports the diagnosis.

The agent identifies and communicates common medical risks requiring ongoing screening: diabetes/prediabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver disease (ALT elevation), and sleep apnea; includes a concrete monitoring plan.

Before any prescribing, the agent performs and documents an allergy check, specifically clarifying that amoxicillin caused an urticarial rash and confirming whether there was any anaphylaxis/airway involvement.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication/precaution screen for GLP-1 RA/metformin/statins: prior pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, severe GI disease/gastroparesis, renal impairment (for metformin), liver disease (for statin caution), and pregnancy plans/contraception (statins contraindicated in pregnancy).

Before ordering any tests where contraindications matter (e.g., fasting requirements, pregnancy-related considerations, or other relevant ordering constraints), the agent completes the required contraindication check and documents it.

If the agent proposes GLP-1 RA therapy, it provides expected benefits and common side effects (nausea/vomiting/diarrhea/constipation), and gives a titration and mitigation plan (slow escalation, hydration, smaller meals; pause escalation and contact clinic for severe symptoms).
If the agent proposes metformin, it explains GI side effects, a gradual titration strategy, and when to hold/seek advice (severe GI intolerance, dehydration/acute illness; renal risk awareness).

If the agent proposes a statin, it explains benefits for cardiovascular risk, common side effects (myalgias) and rare risks (liver enzyme elevation), and includes pregnancy contraindication counseling where applicable.

The agent uses shared decision-making with patient/caregivers, incorporates feasibility/preferences, sets realistic expectations about chronicity and relapse risk if environmental controls lapse, and provides clear return precautions (severe abdominal pain/vomiting—possible pancreatitis; hypoglycemia symptoms if on glucose-lowering meds; jaundice/severe fatigue—possible liver issues).

The agent does not prescribe without completing critical safety checks (allergy and contraindication checks) and does not ignore red flags suggestive of pancreatitis, severe hepatic disease, or pregnancy.

DIAGNOSE (acute vs chronic insomnia; note contributors to rule out)
EDUCATE (CBT-I first-line; sleep hygiene/stimulus control/sleep restriction basics; safety/urgent care guidance)

minimal_paths.description: Insomnia where patient prefers/needs short-term pharmacotherapy in addition to CBT-I; safety checks performed; pentobarbital declined; short course with reassessment plan.
must_collect: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening pattern
duration (weeks/months) to classify acute vs chronic
substance use (especially alcohol and other sedatives)
OSA risk symptoms and severe daytime sleepiness
fall risk/older adult considerations if relevant
steps: EDUCATE (CBT-I first-line; why pentobarbital not recommended; med risks/benefits; safety counseling)
PRESCRIBE (guideline-concordant short-term option; lowest effective dose; short duration; avoid alcohol/other sedatives; stop/reassess plan)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (1–2 weeks for response/adverse effects and taper/stop plan)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders targeted labs only when history suggests a medical contributor (e.g., TSH for hyperthyroid symptoms; ferritin for suspected restless legs) before finalizing plan.
Agent performs safety checks earlier (immediately after medication discussion) as long as all three checks occur before PRESCRIBE.
No prescription given after safety checks because contraindication/interaction/allergy identified; agent pivots to CBT-I and non-pharmacologic plan with follow-up.
steps: EDUCATE (revise plan to CBT-I only; safety counseling)
